meta.title: Axure Cloud: Modern design refresh and platform performance
meta.oneLiner: Refreshed Axure Cloud with a modern design, rebuilt architecture for speed, and shipped seven high-impact features to improve navigation and daily workflow.

overview.companyContext: Axure Cloud is the collaboration and hosting platform for Axure RP and Axure Whiteboards, used by product managers, UX designers, and business analysts to share prototypes, specs, and design files. The platform had accumulated UI debt and performance issues as usage scaled.
overview.mandate: Deliver a major update that modernizes the visual design, improves platform speed and responsiveness, and ships a set of features that make files, workspaces, and settings easier to find and manage—without disrupting existing projects or integrations.
constraints: No breaking changes to existing share links, embeds, or SSO in this release
Design refresh had to feel cohesive across Cloud and RP sign-in flows
Target: ship before end of 2024 to align with annual planning and renewals

myContributions.title: Discovery and prioritization
myContributions.body: Led user research and internal feedback sessions to define the design refresh scope and prioritize the seven improvements (quick search, recent files, comments, workspaces, activity feed, org moves, centralized settings).

myContributions.title: Spec and cross-team alignment
myContributions.body: Wrote PRDs and worked with design and engineering to sequence the architecture rebuild and feature work into a single shippable release.

myContributions.title: Launch and communication
myContributions.body: Coordinated the Dec 10 release, changelog, and blog post to communicate the update clearly to existing customers and prospects.

problem.userProblem: Users found Axure Cloud’s navigation and layout dated and sometimes slow. Locating recent files, switching workspaces, and managing settings required too many clicks; comment discovery and activity visibility were limited.
problem.businessProblem: Support and sales were fielding feedback about speed and “finding things.” Retention and NPS had room to improve, and the product needed to feel modern to compete with other collaboration tools.
problem.whyNow: We had already planned an architecture refresh for performance; leadership approved scope to pair it with a design refresh and a focused set of workflow improvements so one release could move the needle on both perception and daily use.

workflowImage.caption: Pre-refresh Axure Cloud: navigation and layout before the update.

process.researchApproach: We combined support themes, in-app feedback, and sessions with power users and new adopters to map where the experience felt slow or confusing. We also ran internal dogfooding to prioritize which improvements would have the highest impact for the engineering and design effort required.

technoFunctional: Parts of the platform’s architecture had to be rebuilt for performance; we had to scope the rebuild so we could ship on schedule.
Settings and workspace logic were spread across multiple surfaces; we had to consolidate without breaking existing org and permission models.
experiential: Users didn’t have a single way to jump to recent work or search across files and workspaces; we needed quick search and recent files as first-class entry points.
Comment and activity context were hard to find; we needed search, sort, and a revamped activity feed to surface what mattered.

keyDecisions.decision: One major release combining design refresh, performance work, and the seven improvements.
keyDecisions.rationale: Shipping together created a clear “before and after” story, reduced coordination overhead, and let us communicate one cohesive update to users and sales.

keyDecisions.decision: Quick search (Cmd+K / Ctrl+K) and recent files in the sidebar as anchor features.
keyDecisions.rationale: Research showed that “finding my file” and “getting back to recent work” were top friction points; keyboard search and recent files addressed both with minimal new UI.

process.pivots: We initially considered a settings-only consolidation. User feedback showed that discoverability of workspaces and activity was equally important, so we expanded scope to include Browse Workspaces, revamped activity feed, and the ability to move workspaces between organizations.

images.caption: Research themes: navigation, speed, and discoverability.
Prioritized feature set and architecture workstreams.

solution.overview: We shipped a design refresh across Axure Cloud, rebuilt key parts of the platform for better speed and responsiveness, and delivered seven improvements: quick search, recent files in the sidebar, enhanced comments (search and sort), Browse Workspaces, revamped activity feed, move workspaces between organizations, and centralized settings.

baselineImages.caption: Before: previous Cloud layout and navigation.
After: refreshed design with quick search and recent files.

features.title: Quick search and recent files
features.problem: Users had no fast way to find files, folders, or workspaces or to return to recent work.
features.solution: Quick search (Cmd+K on Mac, Ctrl+K on Windows) to locate files, folders, and workspaces; recent files added to the sidebar for quick access.

features.title: Enhanced comments and activity
features.problem: Comments and activity were hard to search, sort, or scan for what mattered.
features.solution: Comment functionality enhanced with search and sort; revamped activity feed to highlight important events.

features.title: Workspaces and settings
features.problem: Discovering and joining workspaces was unclear; settings were scattered; moving workspaces between organizations wasn’t supported.
features.solution: Browse Workspaces to find and join organization workspaces; ability to move workspaces between organizations; centralized settings on a single page.

quantitative: Platform responsiveness and load times improved after the architecture rebuild; we tracked meaningful gains in core Web Vitals.
Quick search and recent files drove higher reuse of the sidebar and fewer “where did I put that?” support questions in the first weeks post-launch.
Centralized settings reduced support tickets related to “where do I change X?” and simplified onboarding for new admins.
qualitative: Internal and beta users reported that the product “feels faster” and “easier to get around.”
Sales and customer success used the changelog and blog post to explain the update clearly; feedback from enterprise accounts was positive.
results.nextSteps: We’re iterating on quick search (e.g. recent queries, filters) and activity feed relevance, and exploring more ways to streamline workspace and org management.
results.retrospective: Combining design refresh, performance work, and the seven improvements in one release was the right call for impact and narrative. Keeping existing links and SSO unchanged avoided risk and let us focus adoption messaging on the new experience. Earlier alignment with docs and support on the centralized settings map would have made launch week smoother.
